South-East & London (Kent / London).note: Core of the Anglo-Saxon south-east; London held the Hanse Steelyard (Stalhof) and the Cinque Ports faced Flanders.
admin1: Royal Borough of Windsor and Maidenhead

Wessex & the South-West.note: Old kingdom of Wessex plus Cornwall/Dumnonia; Southampton and Bristol were the western outlets for wool and Gascon wine.

Mercia & the Midlands.note: Inland kingdom of Mercia and the Danelaw shires; wool-growing hinterland feeding the eastern staple ports.

East Anglia.note: Kingdom of the East Angles; King's Lynn (Bishop's Lynn), Yarmouth and Ipswich were major Hanse grain/wool ports on the Wash and North Sea.

Northumbria & the North.note: Old Northumbria and the northern Danelaw; Hull (Kingston upon Hull) and York shipped Yorkshire wool to the Low Countries and Baltic.

County of Flanders.note: The great cloth county; Bruges hosted the Hanseatic Kontor, the western hub of the whole League, with Ghent and Ypres.

Duchy of Brabant.note: Brabant fairs and the rise of Antwerp as staple; Brussels the ducal seat, North Brabant its northern quarter.

Hainaut, Namur & Liège (the Meuse princi.note: Inland Walloon lands: the county of Hainaut, prince-bishopric of Liège, Namur and Luxembourg — partly framing, south of the Hanse coast.

County of Holland & Zeeland.note: Holland and Zeeland: Amsterdam, Dordrecht and the Zuiderzee ports — Hanse partners and eventually its fiercest maritime rivals; Utrecht was a prince-bishopric.

Guelders & the Oversticht (IJssel Hanse.note: The IJssel/Zuiderzee Hanseatic towns — Kampen, Deventer, Zwolle — in the duchy of Guelders and the bishop of Utrecht's Oversticht (Overijssel, Drenthe).

The Limburgs & Lower Meuse.note: The Meuse marches around Maastricht: the county of Loon (Belgian Limburg, under Liège) and Upper Guelders/Jülich (Dutch Limburg). NB both admin-1 units are literally named "Limburg".

Frisia (Friesland & Groningen).note: The Free Frisian lands and the city of Groningen — never fully feudalised; Groningen dominated the Ommelanden and Frisian coastal trade.

Holstein & Lübeck (Schleswig-Holstein).note: Holstein and the Wagrian coast; Lübeck — "Queen of the Hanse" and capital of the Wendish quarter — lies on this isthmus between North and Baltic Seas.

Hamburg (Free Hanseatic City).note: The Elbe-mouth Hanse metropolis; Hamburg and Lübeck jointly controlled the salt road and the North-Sea–Baltic transit.

Mecklenburg & Pomerania (Vorpommern).note: The Wendish Baltic shore: Wismar and Rostock (Mecklenburg) plus Stralsund, Greifswald and Rügen (Vorpommern) — the militant core of the Wendish towns.

Lower Saxony (Duchy of the Welfs).note: The Welf duchy of Brunswick-Lüneburg: Lüneburg's salt, Brunswick and Hanover — a founding Saxon-Hanse heartland.

Bremen (Free Hanseatic City).note: The Weser-mouth archbishopric and Hanse city; gateway for Westphalian and North-Sea trade.

Old Saxony & Magdeburg (Saxony-Anhalt).note: The Ottonian heartland on the middle Elbe; Magdeburg and its far-copied town law, plus the Harz silver.

Mark Brandenburg.note: The Ascanian march east of the Elbe; Berlin/Cölln and Frankfurt-an-der-Oder were Hanse towns on the road to Poland.

Meissen & Thuringia (the upland hinterla.note: Wettin Upper Saxony (Meissen, Leipzig) and Thuringia (Erfurt) — inland trade-route hinterland, largely framing.

Rhineland & Westphalia (Archbishopric of.note: Cologne — the greatest German city and Rhine staple — with the Westphalian Hanse towns Dortmund, Soest and Münster (one Bundesland spans both historic regions).

Middle Rhine (Rhineland-Palatinate).note: Mainz, Trier, Worms and Koblenz on the middle Rhine and Moselle — the up-river wine and transit trade feeding Cologne.

Hesse (Landgraviate).note: Landgraviate of Hesse; Frankfurt's fairs and the central German overland routes — Hanse-adjacent hinterland.

Zealand (Sjælland).note: The Danish royal core: Copenhagen (which taxed the Sound) and Roskilde; includes Lolland-Falster and Bornholm.

Funen & South Jutland (Fyn / Sønderjylla.note: Funen (Odense) with southern Jutland and the duchy of Schleswig borderland; Ribe was an old North-Sea trade town.

Jutland (Nørrejylland).note: The northern peninsula: Aalborg, Aarhus and Viborg; cattle and herring for the Hanse.

Scania (Skåneland).note: MEDIEVALLY DANISH (Swedish only after 1658): Skåne, Halland and Blekinge; the Scanian herring fairs at Skanör/Falsterbo were the Hanse's greatest market, with Lund the archiepiscopal see.

Viken (the Oslofjord & East).note: The eastern trading region around the Oslofjord: Oslo and Tønsberg, where Hanse (mainly Rostock) merchants were active.

Agder (the South Coast).note: The Skagerrak coast of Agder — timber and small harbours between Viken and the west.

Opplandene (the Interior).note: The inland valleys (Gudbrandsdalen, Hedmark, Oppland) — agricultural hinterland behind Viken.

Vestlandet (the West Country).note: The fjord west: Bergen held the Hanseatic Kontor at Bryggen, controlling the stockfish (dried cod) staple that fed much of Europe.

Trøndelag.note: Trøndelag around Nidaros (Trondheim), Norway's archiepiscopal see and northern grain district.

Hålogaland (the North).note: The far north; Lofoten/Vesterålen stockfish shipped south to Bergen. Troms and Finnmark lie mostly beyond the play-area (framing).

Uppland.note: The royal land north of the Mälaren; Stockholm (founded c.1250) became the capital and iron/copper export port with a large German burgher community; Uppsala the archiepiscopal see.

Södermanland & the Mälaren lands.note: Södermanland, Västmanland and Närke around Lake Mälaren, plus Värmland and the Bergslagen/Dalarna mining country (Falun copper, osmund iron) shipped via Stockholm.

Östergötland.note: The fertile eastern plain; Söderköping was the medieval east-coast staple and Hanse port, with Vadstena its great abbey.

Västergötland.note: The western land toward the Göta river; Lödöse was Sweden's outlet to the North Sea. (Admin-1 also covers Bohuslän and Dalsland, medievally Norwegian borderlands — flag.)

Småland & Öland.note: The forested southern uplands and the island of Öland; Kalmar — great castle and east-coast port, seat of the 1397 Kalmar Union — bordered Danish Scania.

Gotland.note: Visby on Gotland was the pivotal early Hanse hub of the eastern Baltic ("community of German merchants visiting Gotland"); sacked by Denmark 1361 and thereafter Danish.

Norrland (the North).note: The northern forest and Bothnian coast (furs, tar, salmon, iron). Jämtland was medievally Norwegian; Norrbotten lies largely beyond the play-area (framing).

Finland Proper (Varsinais-Suomi).note: The south-west core around Åbo/Turku — cathedral city, bishop's seat and Finland's chief port linked to Stockholm and the Hanse.

Satakunta.note: The Kokemäki-river country north of Turku (Ulvila/Pori); Pirkanmaa (Tampere) formed its inland march with Häme.

Tavastia (Häme).note: The interior lake district dominated by Häme Castle; Päijät-Häme and Central Finland its northern extensions.

Uusimaa (Nyland).note: The Gulf-of-Finland coast (Porvoo/Borgå, later Helsinki); Kymenlaakso its eastern, Karelian-facing march.

Savonia (Savo).note: The eastern lake wilderness colonised from Häme; Olofsborg/Savonlinna (1475) guarded the frontier with Novgorod. Kainuu was long a disputed northern wilderness.

Karelia (Karjala).note: The Novgorod frontier; Viborg/Viipuri, founded 1293, was the great eastern fortress and fur-trade town of the Swedish realm.

Ostrobothnia (Österbotten).note: The Bothnian coast; tar, furs, salmon and stockfish from Vaasa/Mustasaari up to Oulu.

Kurzeme (Courland).note: The duchy of Courland west of the Gulf of Riga; the Livonian Order's Kurland bailiwick, with the ports of Windau (Ventspils), Libau (Liepāja) and Goldingen (Kuldīga).

Zemgale (Semigallia).note: The Semigallian plain south of Riga (Mitau/Jelgava, Bauske); fertile Lielupe grain country, long a pagan-Baltic frontier.

Sēlija (Selonia).note: Selonia, south of the Daugava between Semigallia and Latgale (Jēkabpils, Ilūkste). Often merged into Zemgale — flag for review.

Vidzeme (Livonia proper).note: The core of medieval "Livland" north of the Daugava: Riga (Hanse city, archbishop's and Order's seat) and Wenden/Cēsis, capital of the Livonian Order.

Latgale (Latgallia).note: Latgallia in the far south-east (Rēzekne, Ludza, Dünaburg/Daugavpils); the Order's eastern march facing Pskov and Polotsk.

Samogitia (Žemaitija).note: The western lowlands the Teutonic Order fought Lithuania for; Memel/Klaipėda was actually Order territory in the Hansa era (flag). Šiauliai and Telšiai are the Samogitian heartland.

Aukštaitija (the Highlands).note: The upper Neris/Nemunas highlands north-east of Kaunas — the old ethnographic core of the Lithuanians.

Kaunas & the Nemunas valley.note: Kaunas/Kauen at the Neris–Nemunas confluence; a river trade town that hosted a Hanseatic office in the 15th century, on the road from Danzig up the Nemunas.

Vilnius (Lithuania Proper).note: The grand-ducal capital founded c.1323; seat of the Gediminid dynasty and hub of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania.

Sūduva & Dzūkija (the southern forests).note: Sudovia/Užnemunė west of the Nemunas (old Yotvingian land) and Dzūkija to the south-east — sparsely settled forest borderland toward Prussia and Poland.

Novgorod the Great.note: The Novgorod Republic; the Hanseatic Peterhof (St Peter's Court) was the League's easternmost Kontor, trading furs, wax and honey for cloth.

Ingria & Ladoga (Vodskaya & Korela).note: Novgorod's north-western pyatiny on the Neva and Lake Ladoga (Oreshek/Nöteborg); the fur/fish frontier contested with Sweden. (St Petersburg is a later foundation on this ground.)

Pskov.note: The Pskov Republic; a walled Hanse trading town (the Zavelichye German quarter) on the front line with the Livonian Order.

Polotsk & Vitebsk.note: The principality of Polotsk on the Western Dvina/Daugava — terminus of the Riga river route into Rus'; Vitebsk and Polotsk drew Hanse cloth up the Dvina. (Belarus admin-1.)

Pomerelia (Danzig).note: Gdańsk/Danzig — the premier Baltic Hanse port at the Vistula mouth; Pomerelia was seized by the Teutonic Order in 1308, then Royal Prussia after 1466. Marienburg/Malbork, the Order's capital, lies here.

Culmerland & Warmia (the Order's heartla.note: Thorn/Toruń and Kulm/Chełmno — the first Hanse towns of the Ordensstaat — with Warmia (the prince-bishopric) and Masuria. NB the Kuyavian-Pomeranian unit also covers Polish Kuyavia in its south (split unit — flag).

East Prussia / Sambia (Königsberg).note: Königsberg (Kaliningrad) — Order capital from 1457 and a Hanse town — with the Samland peninsula and the amber coast. (Belongs to Russia today.)

Greater Poland (Wielkopolska).note: The cradle of the Polish state — Poznań and Gniezno — with the Lubusz (Lebus) land on the Oder and the Sieradz-Łęczyca march (Łódź).

Masovia (Mazowsze).note: The duchy of Masovia (Warsaw, Płock), only fully absorbed into the Crown in 1526; Podlasie was a Lithuanian-Mazovian borderland (part of the Grand Duchy until 1569 — flag).

Lesser Poland (Małopolska).note: Kraków — the royal capital — with Sandomierz and Lublin; the upper Vistula grain and Kraków–Thorn trade route ran down to Danzig.

Silesia (Śląsk).note: Wrocław/Breslau was a major Hanse city, but Silesia passed to the Bohemian (Luxembourg) crown after 1335 — Imperial, not Polish, in the Hansa era (flag).

Pomerania (Stettin / Farther Pomerania).note: Szczecin/Stettin and Kolberg — the Baltic coast of the duchy of Pomerania (Wolgast/Stettin), an Imperial fief and Hanse shore, not a Polish-crown land medievally (flag).

Netherlands (Caribbean).note: Caribbean municipalities, far outside the map.

Germany (South).note: South Germany beyond the Hanse realms.

Finland (Lapland).note: The far north, almost entirely beyond the play-area (lat > 66).

Belarus (Grand Duchy interior).note: Interior of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania / Ruthenia, beyond the coastal realms.

Russia (beyond Novgorod scope).note: Russian oblasts east/south of the Novgorod scope; Bryansk, Smolensk, Tver' and Karelia only clip the play-area edge, their centres lie beyond. Chukchi appears as a false positive (its geometry crosses the antimeridian). All other Russian units are fully out of the map.
